summary: The pulmonary artery contains blood immediately prior to gas exchange, making its oxygen content lower than any systemically derived vessel like the inferior vena cava due to lack of recent exposure to alveolar air during transit through lung tissue. Despite similarities between mixed venous and pulmonic arterial blood levels pre-gas exchange, physiological data indicates that pulmonary arteries have slightly reduced oxygen concentration relative to central venae such as the inferior vena cava owing to minor regional differences within circulatory flow dynamics leading up to perfusion sites where initial diffusion begins occurring early stages near bronchopulmonary junctions affecting local partial pressures just beyond entrance point into vascular bed itself influencing final readings taken directly downstream from arterioles feeding terminal segments involved in actual transfer processes across respiratory membranes thus resulting ultimately in marginally decreased values observed when comparing these two types of vessels under resting conditions particularly noticeable upon closer inspection considering subtle variations introduced throughout journey preceding arrival inside primary capillary networks located deep within
